concepts.name: Conv2D
bullets: is a convolutional layer that applies learnable 2D filters (kernels) over spatial dimensions of an input feature map
has parameter count given by (k_h × k_w × C_in + 1) × C_out when using a bias term, where k_h and k_w are kernel height and width, C_in is input channels, and C_out is output channels
computes each output feature as y[n, k, i, j] = Σ_c Σ_u Σ_v w[k, c, u, v] · x[n, c, i + u, j + v] + b[k]
has spatial output size (H_out, W_out) given by H_out = ⌊(H_in + 2·pad_h − k_h) / stride_h⌋ + 1 and W_out = ⌊(W_in + 2·pad_w − k_w) / stride_w⌋ + 1
has the effect of extracting local patterns such as edges, textures, and shapes by sliding kernels over height and width
shares filter weights across spatial locations, making it more parameter-efficient than fully connected layers for images
is usually followed by a non-linear activation function and sometimes normalization

concepts.name: Max Pooling
bullets: is a downsampling operation that outputs the maximum value within each local window of the input feature map
contains no learnable parameters; it only applies a fixed max operation
for a window of size k_h × k_w, computes y[n, c, i, j] = max_{0 ≤ u < k_h, 0 ≤ v < k_w} x[n, c, i + u, j + v]
reduces spatial dimensions H and W while keeping the number of channels C unchanged
helps achieve translation invariance by making small spatial shifts in the input produce similar outputs
can act as a form of regularization by discarding less active responses

concepts.name: Average Pooling
bullets: is a downsampling operation that outputs the arithmetic mean of values within each local window
contains no learnable parameters; it only averages values in each pooling window
for a window of size k_h × k_w, computes y[n, c, i, j] = (1 / (k_h · k_w)) · Σ_{0 ≤ u < k_h} Σ_{0 ≤ v < k_w} x[n, c, i + u, j + v]
reduces spatial dimensions H and W without changing the number of channels C
tends to produce smoother feature maps compared to max pooling because it aggregates all values
is often used as global average pooling at the end of CNNs to collapse each channel to a single scalar

concepts.name: Batch Normalization
bullets: is a normalization layer that standardizes activations per mini-batch along specific dimensions
for C channels, learns 2 × C parameters in total: a scale γ_c and shift β_c for each channel c
computes batch statistics μ_c = (1/m) · Σ_i x_{i,c} and σ_c^2 = (1/m) · Σ_i (x_{i,c} − μ_c)^2, where m is the number of elements for that channel in the batch
normalizes activations as x̂_{i,c} = (x_{i,c} − μ_c) / √(σ_c^2 + ε) and then applies y_{i,c} = γ_c · x̂_{i,c} + β_c
reduces internal covariate shift by stabilizing the distribution of activations during training
often allows the use of higher learning rates and speeds up convergence
behaves differently in training and evaluation modes, using batch statistics during training and running estimates during evaluation
acts as a mild regularizer but is not a replacement for explicit regularization like dropout or weight decay

concepts.name: Dropout
bullets: is a regularization technique that randomly sets a fraction of activations to zero during training
contains no learnable parameters; its behavior is controlled only by the dropout probability p
during training, samples a binary mask m with P(m_i = 0) = p and computes y = (m ⊙ x) / (1 − p) in inverted-dropout implementations
is disabled in evaluation mode so that all activations pass through without being dropped
helps prevent overfitting by discouraging co-adaptation of neurons and promoting redundancy
is typically used in fully connected layers and sometimes in convolutional layers with care

concepts.name: Activation Functions (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh, GELU)
bullets: include pointwise non-linearities applied after linear or convolutional layers to increase model capacity
ReLU is defined as ReLU(x) = max(0, x)
Sigmoid is defined as σ(x) = 1 / (1 + exp(−x)) and outputs values in (0, 1)
Tanh is defined as tanh(x) = (exp(x) − exp(−x)) / (exp(x) + exp(−x)) and outputs values in (−1, 1)
GELU is often approximated as GELU(x) ≈ 0.5 · x · (1 + tanh(√(2/π) · (x + 0.044715 · x^3)))
are important to recognize both by formula and by typical graph shape for exam questions

concepts.name: Fully Connected (Linear) Layer
bullets: is a dense layer where every input unit connects to every output unit via a weight
has parameters organized in a weight matrix of shape [out_features, in_features] plus an optional bias vector
treats all input features equally without regard to spatial structure
is typically used after convolutional and pooling layers for classification or regression heads
can lead to many parameters for high-dimensional inputs, which can encourage overfitting if not controlled

concepts.name: LeNet
bullets: is one of the earliest convolutional neural network architectures designed for digit recognition
has a relatively shallow structure with alternating convolution, pooling, and fully connected layers
uses small grayscale inputs and simple convolutions compared to modern deep networks
demonstrated that learned convolutional features outperform handcrafted features for vision tasks
laid the foundation for later large-scale CNN architectures like AlexNet and VGG

concepts.name: AlexNet
bullets: is a deep CNN that achieved a breakthrough result on the ImageNet classification challenge
has multiple convolutional layers followed by fully connected layers, using ReLU activations extensively
introduced heavy use of data augmentation, dropout, and GPU training for large-scale vision
uses large first-layer kernels and overlapping max pooling in early layers
helped popularize deep learning for computer vision in practice

concepts.name: VGG
bullets: is a CNN architecture that uses a very uniform design with many stacked 3x3 convolutions
has deep variants (e.g., VGG-16, VGG-19) with a large number of layers and parameters
relies on repeated blocks of conv-conv-pool to reduce spatial size and increase channels
achieves strong performance but is computationally expensive and memory heavy
is often used as a backbone for transfer learning, especially in earlier works

concepts.name: GoogLeNet / Inception
bullets: is a CNN family that introduced Inception modules with multiple parallel convolutional paths
uses 1x1 convolutions extensively for dimensionality reduction and computational efficiency
combines convolutions with different kernel sizes in parallel to capture multi-scale features
reduces parameter count compared to very deep uniform stacks like VGG
popularized the idea of carefully designed modules instead of naive depth increase

concepts.name: ResNet
bullets: is a residual network architecture that uses skip connections (identity mappings) across blocks
addresses vanishing gradients and degradation when making networks very deep
models each block as learning a residual function relative to its input
has variants with different depths (e.g., ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-101) built from residual blocks or bottleneck blocks
became a standard backbone for many tasks including detection, segmentation, and recognition

concepts.name: DenseNet
bullets: is a CNN architecture where each layer within a block receives as input the concatenation of all previous layers' feature maps
promotes feature reuse and encourages implicit deep supervision through dense connections
tends to require fewer parameters because it can use narrower layers while maintaining rich representations
introduces growth rate and transition layers to control channel dimensionality and downsampling
can improve gradient flow and generalization compared to plain feed-forward stacks

concepts.name: MobileNet
bullets: is a family of lightweight CNNs designed for mobile and embedded devices
relies heavily on depthwise separable convolutions to drastically reduce computation and parameters
introduces width multipliers and resolution multipliers to trade off accuracy versus efficiency
is often used as a backbone in real-time detection and segmentation models on resource-constrained hardware

concepts.name: EfficientNet
bullets: is a CNN family that scales depth, width, and resolution in a principled, compound way
starts from a small baseline network and applies fixed scaling coefficients to obtain larger variants
achieves strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs compared to earlier CNNs
is frequently used in image classification benchmarks and as a backbone for downstream tasks

concepts.name: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
bullets: is an optimization algorithm that updates model parameters using gradients computed on small mini-batches instead of the full dataset
has a learning rate hyperparameter that controls the step size of each parameter update
introduces stochasticity because each mini-batch provides a noisy estimate of the true gradient
can escape shallow local minima and saddle points thanks to gradient noise
is simple to implement and widely used as a baseline optimizer
can be enhanced with momentum, weight decay, and learning rate schedules for better convergence

concepts.name: SGD with Momentum
bullets: is a variant of SGD that accumulates an exponential moving average of past gradients
has a momentum coefficient that controls how much of the previous update direction is retained
helps smooth out noisy gradient directions and accelerates learning along consistent slopes
can reduce oscillations in narrow valleys of the loss landscape
often converges faster and more stably than vanilla SGD with the same learning rate

concepts.name: Adam Optimizer
bullets: is an adaptive optimization algorithm that maintains moving averages of both gradients and squared gradients
has hyperparameters beta1 and beta2 that control the decay rates of the first and second moment estimates
scales each parameter’s update by an estimate of its past gradient magnitude, resulting in per-parameter learning rates
often converges faster than SGD in practice, especially for complex models and sparse gradients
includes an epsilon term to improve numerical stability when dividing by small values
is widely used for training transformers, CNNs, and many modern deep learning architectures

concepts.name: Learning Rate
bullets: is a key hyperparameter that determines the magnitude of each parameter update step
has to be chosen carefully because too large values can cause divergence and NaNs
causes very slow convergence or training stagnation if set too small
often benefits from scheduling strategies that reduce it over time as training progresses
can be tuned using heuristics, cyclic policies, or learning rate range tests

concepts.name: Learning Rate Schedule
bullets: is a predefined or adaptive rule that changes the learning rate across training steps or epochs
has common forms such as step decay, exponential decay, cosine annealing, and cyclic schedules
helps the optimizer take larger steps early in training and smaller, more precise steps later
can improve both convergence speed and final generalization performance
is often combined with warm-up phases in very deep networks or transformers

concepts.name: Cosine Annealing Schedule
bullets: is a learning rate schedule where the learning rate follows a cosine-shaped decay curve over a training period
starts from a maximum learning rate and gradually decreases it towards a minimum value following a smooth cosine function
can optionally be combined with restarts to cycle between high and low learning rates
aims to encourage exploration early on and fine-tuning near the end of training
is frequently used in modern training pipelines for both CNNs and transformers

concepts.name: Learning Rate Warm-up
bullets: is a strategy that starts training with a smaller learning rate and gradually increases it to a target value over several steps or epochs
helps stabilize early training when gradients and activations can be unstable
is particularly important for very deep or large-batch models such as transformers and large CNNs
can prevent divergence that would occur if using the full learning rate from the first iteration
is usually followed by a decay schedule once the warm-up phase finishes

concepts.name: Batch Size
bullets: is the number of samples used to compute one gradient update in mini-batch training
affects the noise level of the gradient estimate, with smaller batches producing noisier gradients
impacts memory usage on hardware accelerators because larger batches require more VRAM
can influence generalization, where very large batches may sometimes lead to poorer test performance
is often chosen as a compromise between stability, generalization, and computational efficiency

concepts.name: Overfitting
bullets: is a phenomenon where a model fits the training data very well but fails to generalize to unseen data
often manifests as decreasing training loss with increasing validation loss after some point
is more likely when the model has high capacity relative to the amount of training data
can be mitigated with regularization techniques such as dropout, weight decay, and data augmentation
can also be addressed by early stopping when validation performance stops improving

concepts.name: Underfitting
bullets: occurs when a model is too simple or poorly trained to capture the underlying patterns in the data
is typically characterized by high training loss and poor performance on both training and validation sets
can be caused by insufficient training time, inadequate model capacity, or excessively strong regularization
can be mitigated by increasing model complexity, training longer, or relaxing regularization constraints
signals that the model has not yet reached its optimal representational potential

concepts.name: Gradient Clipping
bullets: is a technique that limits the magnitude of gradients during backpropagation
is commonly implemented by scaling gradients when their norm exceeds a chosen threshold
helps prevent exploding gradients that can destabilize training and produce NaN values
is especially useful in very deep networks and recurrent architectures
allows the optimizer to maintain stable updates even when encountering large error signals

concepts.name: Weight Decay (L2 Regularization)
bullets: is a regularization method that penalizes large weights by adding their squared magnitude to the loss function
is often implemented in SGD as subtracting a small fraction of the current weight value at each update step
encourages simpler models by shrinking parameters toward zero over time
helps reduce overfitting by discouraging overly complex parameter configurations
is commonly used together with SGD or Adam to improve generalization

concepts.name: Cross-Entropy Loss (Categorical)
bullets: is a loss function used for multi-class classification where each example belongs to exactly one class
measures the distance between the predicted probability distribution and the one-hot ground-truth distribution
is minimized when the model assigns high probability to the correct class and low probability to others
is typically used with a Softmax output layer in single-label classification problems
can be extended to class-weighted versions when handling class imbalance

concepts.name: Binary Cross-Entropy Loss
bullets: is a loss function used for binary classification or independent multi-label classification tasks
is defined per output as L = − [ y · log(ŷ) + (1 − y) · log(1 − ŷ) ] where ŷ is a predicted probability
is often applied with a Sigmoid activation for each output unit
treats each label as an independent binary decision in multi-label classification
can be adapted with positive or negative class weighting to handle imbalance

concepts.name: Binary Cross-Entropy in Questions Context
bullets: is the same loss used in your exam questions where the formula is written as − [y log(ŷ) + (1 − y) log(1 − ŷ)]
implements a per-pixel or per-label penalty in segmentation and multi-label classification settings
aligns exactly with the binary cross-entropy formulations appearing in your notebook and labs

concepts.name: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
bullets: is a regression loss that computes the average of squared differences between predictions and targets
is given by L = (1/N) · Σ_i (ŷ_i − y_i)^2 over N samples or elements
penalizes larger errors more heavily because the error term is squared
is sensitive to outliers since large deviations contribute disproportionately to the loss
is commonly used when predicting continuous quantities such as coordinates or intensities
has a smooth gradient that encourages predictions to move toward the mean of the targets

concepts.name: Mean Absolute Error (MAE / L1 Loss)
bullets: is a regression loss that computes the average of absolute differences between predictions and targets
is given by L = (1/N) · Σ_i |ŷ_i − y_i| over N samples or elements
penalizes all errors linearly, making it more robust to outliers than MSE
has gradients that are constant in magnitude away from zero error
can lead to median-like behavior where the model predicts median values of the target distribution
is sometimes combined with MSE in composite losses to balance robustness and smoothness

concepts.name: Hinge Loss
bullets: is a margin-based loss commonly used with linear classifiers like SVMs
is defined as L = max(0, 1 − y · ŷ) where y ∈ {−1, 1} and ŷ is the raw score
penalizes predictions that are on the wrong side of the decision boundary or too close to it
encourages a large margin between positive and negative examples
is less common in modern deep networks than cross-entropy but still useful in some margin-based setups

concepts.name: Dice Loss / IoU-based Losses (context)
bullets: are overlap-based losses designed for segmentation tasks with imbalanced foreground and background
measure similarity between predicted and ground-truth masks using set overlap rather than per-pixel averages
are more robust than plain cross-entropy when the positive class occupies a small fraction of the image
can be combined with cross-entropy to balance region overlap and per-pixel classification quality
are commonly used in medical imaging and other domains with small structures

concepts.name: Class Imbalance in Loss Functions
bullets: refers to situations where some classes have far fewer training examples or pixels than others
can cause standard cross-entropy to bias predictions toward the majority class
is often addressed by using class weights, focal loss, or overlap-based losses
requires careful evaluation using metrics like Precision, Recall, and IoU rather than Accuracy alone

concepts.name: Precision
bullets: is an evaluation metric that measures how many predicted positives are actually correct
is defined as Precision = TP / (TP + FP)
is high when the model makes few false positive errors
is important in applications where false alarms are costly, such as medical diagnosis or detection of rare events
complements Recall by focusing on prediction quality among positive predictions

concepts.name: Recall
bullets: is an evaluation metric that measures how many actual positives are successfully detected by the model
is defined as Recall = TP / (TP + FN)
is high when the model misses few true positive instances
is important in applications where missing a positive instance is very costly, such as safety-critical detection
often trades off with Precision depending on the chosen decision threshold

concepts.name: F1-Score
bullets: is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall
is defined as F1 = 2 · (P · R) / (P + R) where P is Precision and R is Recall
provides a single number that balances both false positives and false negatives
is especially useful on imbalanced datasets where Accuracy can be misleading
can be computed per class or as a macro/micro average across classes

concepts.name: Accuracy
bullets: is a metric that measures the fraction of correctly classified samples out of all samples
is defined as (True Positives + True Negatives) divided by the total number of samples
can be misleading in highly imbalanced datasets where the majority class dominates
is appropriate when all classes are balanced and errors have similar costs
is often reported together with more informative metrics like Precision, Recall, and F1

concepts.name: Intersection over Union (IoU)
bullets: is a metric that quantifies the overlap between a predicted region (box or mask) and the ground-truth region
is computed as IoU = |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B| where A and B are the predicted and ground-truth regions
ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap)
is used as a threshold criterion to decide if a predicted box is a True Positive in detection
is also used directly as a score for segmentation mask quality

concepts.name: Average Precision (AP)
bullets: is a detection metric that summarizes the Precision-Recall curve for a single class
is computed as the area under the Precision-Recall curve, often approximated by a sum over recall steps: AP = Σ_k (R_k − R_{k−1}) · P_envelope(k)
depends on a chosen IoU threshold that defines True Positives
is sensitive to both localization quality and ranking of detections by confidence
provides a more detailed view than a single Precision or Recall value at one threshold

concepts.name: mean Average Precision (mAP)
bullets: is the mean of Average Precision values computed across multiple classes and sometimes across multiple IoU thresholds
is a standard benchmark metric on datasets like COCO and PASCAL VOC
captures both classification and localization performance in detection tasks
encourages models to rank correct detections ahead of incorrect ones across the entire dataset
is often reported at specific IoU values (e.g., mAP@0.5) or averaged over a range (e.g., mAP@[0.5:0.95])

concepts.name: Self-Attention
bullets: is a mechanism where each token in a sequence attends to all other tokens to gather contextual information
uses query, key, and value vectors derived from the same input embeddings
computes attention weights from similarities between queries and keys, then applies them to values
captures long-range dependencies without relying on recurrence or fixed convolutional receptive fields
is the core building block of transformer architectures for NLP and vision

concepts.name: Multi-Head Attention
bullets: is an extension of self-attention that uses multiple attention heads in parallel
projects the input into different subspaces to learn diverse relationships between tokens
concatenates the outputs of individual heads and applies a final linear projection
allows the model to jointly attend to information at different positions and representation subspaces
improves expressiveness compared to single-head attention with similar total dimension

concepts.name: Scaled Dot-Product Attention
bullets: is the standard attention computation used in transformers
computes attention scores as dot products between queries and keys divided by the square root of the key dimension
applies a Softmax to obtain normalized attention weights over keys for each query
uses the scaling factor to prevent overly large dot products that could saturate Softmax
produces a weighted sum of value vectors as the final contextual representation

concepts.name: Positional Encoding
bullets: is a mechanism to inject information about token order into transformer models
is needed because pure self-attention is permutation-invariant and does not encode sequence position by itself
can be implemented as fixed sinusoidal functions or as learnable positional embeddings
is added to input token embeddings before feeding them into transformer layers
allows the model to distinguish between different positions in a sequence or spatial grid

concepts.name: Transformer Encoder
bullets: is a stack of layers each containing multi-head self-attention and position-wise feed-forward sublayers
uses residual connections and normalization around each sublayer
processes the entire input sequence in parallel to produce contextualized token representations
typically employs bidirectional attention where tokens can attend to both past and future positions
is used in models like BERT and Vision Transformers for understanding tasks

concepts.name: Transformer Decoder
bullets: is a stack of layers that contain masked self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, and feed-forward sublayers
uses causal masking in self-attention so that each position can only attend to previous positions in the output sequence
includes cross-attention to attend over encoder outputs when used in sequence-to-sequence tasks
is used in models like GPT (decoder-only) and the decoder part of the original transformer for generation tasks
supports autoregressive generation by predicting one token at a time conditioned on previous outputs

concepts.name: Vision Transformer (ViT)
bullets: is a model that applies the transformer encoder architecture directly to image patches
splits an image into fixed-size non-overlapping patches and flattens them into vectors
projects each patch vector into an embedding space and adds positional encodings
prepends a special class token whose final representation is used for classification
relies on large-scale pretraining and data augmentation to achieve strong performance on vision tasks

concepts.name: Semantic Segmentation
bullets: is a pixel-level prediction task where each pixel is assigned a semantic class label
does not distinguish between different instances of the same class, treating them as one category
produces dense label maps with the same spatial resolution as the input (or upsampled to it)
is used in applications like road scene understanding and medical imaging
is often modeled with fully convolutional networks and encoder-decoder architectures

concepts.name: Instance Segmentation
bullets: is a task that assigns both a class label and a unique instance identity to each object pixel
separates different objects of the same class into distinct masks
combines aspects of detection (instances) and segmentation (pixel-level regions)
is more complex than semantic segmentation because it must handle object counting and separation
is commonly addressed by models like Mask R-CNN and related architectures

concepts.name: Panoptic Segmentation
bullets: is a unified segmentation task that combines semantic segmentation of stuff (background regions) with instance segmentation of things (object instances)
assigns every pixel a semantic label and, for instances, an associated instance ID
requires consistent handling of both amorphous regions like sky and countable objects like cars
provides a holistic scene understanding in a single output representation
is evaluated with specialized metrics that account for both semantic and instance quality

concepts.name: Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)
bullets: is a network that replaces fully connected layers with convolutions to produce spatially dense outputs
can take input images of arbitrary size and produce correspondingly sized output maps
typically upsamples coarse feature maps using deconvolution or interpolation to match input resolution
was one of the first architectures to adapt classification CNNs for segmentation tasks
serves as a base idea for many later encoder-decoder segmentation models

concepts.name: U-Net
bullets: is an encoder-decoder segmentation architecture with symmetric contracting and expanding paths
uses skip connections between encoder and decoder layers at corresponding spatial resolutions
preserves fine-grained spatial information by concatenating encoder features into decoder stages
was originally proposed for biomedical segmentation but is widely used in many domains
achieves accurate localization with relatively few training images

concepts.name: DeepLab
bullets: is a family of segmentation models that leverage atrous (dilated) convolutions to control receptive field sizes
uses Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) to capture multi-scale context with parallel dilated convolutions
often incorporates strong backbones like ResNet or Xception
balances detailed boundary localization with large-context understanding
has variants such as DeepLabv2, DeepLabv3, and DeepLabv3+ with progressively improved designs

concepts.name: Two-Stage Object Detectors
bullets: are detectors that first generate candidate regions and then classify and refine them in a second stage
use region proposal mechanisms such as Region Proposal Networks (RPN) in Faster R-CNN
tend to achieve high accuracy but can be slower than one-stage methods
allow more complex processing per region, including mask prediction in extensions like Mask R-CNN
separate localization and classification steps conceptually

concepts.name: One-Stage Object Detectors
bullets: are detectors that directly predict class probabilities and bounding box offsets in a single pass over the image
include models like YOLO and SSD that are optimized for speed
treat detection as a dense prediction problem over a set of predefined anchor boxes or grid cells
are well-suited for real-time applications where latency is critical
have evolved through multiple versions to narrow the accuracy gap to two-stage methods

concepts.name: YOLO (You Only Look Once)
bullets: is a family of one-stage object detectors designed for real-time performance
divides the image into grid cells that each predict bounding boxes and class scores
performs detection in a single forward pass without a separate proposal stage
balances speed and accuracy through architecture and anchor design choices
has multiple versions (e.g., YOLOv3, YOLOv5, YOLOv8) with progressively improved backbones and heads

concepts.name: SSD (Single Shot MultiBox Detector)
bullets: is a one-stage detector that predicts class scores and box offsets for a set of default boxes at multiple feature map scales
places anchor boxes of different aspect ratios at each location on several feature maps
handles objects of various sizes by using both high-resolution and low-resolution feature maps
offers a good trade-off between speed and accuracy for many detection tasks
uses hard negative mining in training to balance positive and negative examples

concepts.name: Anchor Boxes
bullets: are predefined bounding boxes with fixed scales and aspect ratios placed at specific positions on feature maps
serve as reference shapes that detectors can adjust via predicted offsets to match ground-truth boxes
allow the network to handle objects of varying sizes and shapes with a limited set of templates
are assigned to ground-truth boxes based on IoU thresholds during training
influence detection performance and must be chosen carefully for each dataset

concepts.name: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)
bullets: is a post-processing algorithm that removes redundant, overlapping detections
keeps the highest-confidence box for each object and suppresses boxes with high IoU overlap with it
reduces multiple detections of the same object down to a single bounding box
uses IoU thresholds to decide which boxes to suppress
is essential for obtaining clean final detection outputs from dense predictions

concepts.name: Saliency Maps
bullets: are gradient-based visualizations that show how sensitive a model’s output is to each input pixel
compute derivatives of the class score with respect to input pixels to form a saliency value
highlight regions where small changes in pixel values most affect the output
can be noisy and high-frequency but provide fine-grained importance information
help interpret what parts of the image drive a particular prediction

concepts.name: Grad-CAM
bullets: is a class-discriminative localization technique for visualizing deep network decisions
uses gradients of a target class score with respect to feature maps in a convolutional layer
aggregates gradients to compute importance weights for each channel in the feature maps
produces a coarse heatmap over the spatial dimensions of the chosen feature maps
upsamples the heatmap to input resolution and overlays it on the image for inspection

concepts.name: Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)
bullets: is a paradigm where models learn useful representations from unlabeled data using automatically generated supervisory signals
defines pretext tasks that do not require manual annotation, such as predicting rotations or solving patch order
aims to produce embeddings that transfer well to downstream supervised tasks with limited labeled data
can significantly reduce the need for large labeled datasets in practice
is widely used in both vision and language domains for pretraining encoders

concepts.name: Contrastive Learning
bullets: is an SSL approach that trains models to bring representations of related samples closer and push unrelated ones apart
uses positive pairs such as two augmented views of the same image and negative pairs from other images
often employs a contrastive loss like InfoNCE over large batches or memory banks
encourages the encoder to capture invariant features that are stable under augmentations
underlies methods like SimCLR, MoCo, and related contrastive frameworks

concepts.name: Masked Prediction / Masked Autoencoders
bullets: are SSL methods that mask out parts of the input and train the model to reconstruct the missing content
apply heavy masking to image patches or tokens to force the encoder to understand global structure
often use a lightweight decoder that reconstructs pixels or features from encoded visible tokens
have been particularly successful with transformer-based vision models like MAE
learn representations that capture high-level semantics from reconstruction signals

concepts.name: Multimodal Learning
bullets: is the learning paradigm that integrates information from multiple modalities such as text, images, audio, and video
aims to learn joint representations that capture relationships across modalities
enables tasks like image-caption retrieval, visual question answering, and cross-modal retrieval
often uses encoders for each modality followed by fusion or alignment mechanisms
benefits from large-scale paired datasets such as image-text pairs

concepts.name: CLIP
bullets: is a model that learns joint image and text embeddings using contrastive pretraining on large-scale image-text pairs
uses separate encoders for images and text that map inputs into a shared embedding space
trains with a contrastive loss to maximize similarity of matching pairs and minimize similarity of mismatched pairs
enables zero-shot classification by comparing image embeddings with embeddings of textual class descriptions
demonstrates strong transfer capabilities across many vision tasks without task-specific training

concepts.name: Cross-Modal Attention
bullets: is an attention mechanism where queries from one modality attend to keys and values from another modality
enables a model to selectively focus on relevant regions in an image given a text query, or vice versa
is used in architectures for tasks like visual question answering and image captioning
generalizes the encoder-decoder cross-attention pattern to heterogeneous inputs
helps fuse information across different sensory streams in a structured way

concepts.name: Tensor Shape Conventions (Vision)
bullets: refer to the standard ordering of dimensions in vision models, commonly [N, C, H, W] in PyTorch
use N for batch size, C for number of channels, H for height, and W for width
are crucial to understand when applying operations like convolutions, pooling, and normalization
must be handled carefully when reshaping, permuting, or flattening tensors
can differ between frameworks (e.g., some use [N, H, W, C]) requiring explicit conversion

concepts.name: Broadcasting (PyTorch / NumPy)
bullets: is a set of rules that allow operations on tensors of different shapes by automatically expanding dimensions
compares shapes from right to left and treats missing or singleton dimensions as broadcastable
avoids explicit replication of data in memory by reusing values along broadcasted dimensions
is heavily used in elementwise operations, normalization, and loss computations
can cause subtle bugs if shapes are not carefully checked before operations

concepts.name: view / reshape
bullets: are operations that change the shape of a tensor without changing its underlying data when possible
require that the total number of elements remain the same before and after the reshape
are often used to flatten convolutional feature maps into vectors before fully connected layers
can fail if the tensor is not contiguous in memory, in which case a contiguous copy may be required
must be used carefully to avoid unintentionally mixing batch and feature dimensions

concepts.name: unsqueeze / squeeze
bullets: are operations that add or remove singleton dimensions (dimensions of size 1)
unsqueeze inserts a new dimension of size 1 at a specified position in the tensor shape
squeeze removes dimensions of size 1, either all of them or at a specified index
are useful for aligning tensor shapes for broadcasting or feeding into layers expecting certain ranks
do not change the underlying data, only the view of the tensor's shape

concepts.name: Softmax along dimension
bullets: is an operation that converts raw scores into probabilities along a specified axis
normalizes values so that they are non-negative and sum to 1 along that dimension
is typically applied along the class dimension in classification logits
is sensitive to large magnitude inputs and is often combined with stability tricks like subtracting the max
plays a role in attention mechanisms where it is applied over similarity scores

concepts.name: numel / size in PyTorch
bullets: numel returns the total number of elements in a tensor across all dimensions
size or shape returns the length of each dimension individually
are essential for verifying shapes before reshaping or for computing parameter counts
help debug broadcasting and dimension mismatch errors in model code

concepts.name: Depthwise Convolution
bullets: is a convolution operation where each input channel is convolved with its own separate spatial filter
reduces computation by removing cross-channel mixing within the spatial convolution step
produces an output with the same number of channels as the input when using one filter per channel
is typically followed by a separate pointwise (1x1) convolution to mix information across channels
is a key building block in depthwise separable convolutions used by MobileNet-like architectures

concepts.name: Pointwise Convolution (1x1 Conv)
bullets: is a convolution with kernel size 1x1 that operates independently at each spatial location
mixes information across channels without changing spatial dimensions
is often used for dimensionality reduction or expansion in CNNs
acts as the second stage in depthwise separable convolutions to combine depthwise outputs
can significantly reduce parameter count when combined with depthwise convolutions

concepts.name: Depthwise Separable Convolution
bullets: is a factorized convolution consisting of a depthwise convolution followed by a pointwise (1x1) convolution
reduces computational cost compared to standard convolutions by separating spatial filtering and channel mixing
is heavily used in lightweight architectures like MobileNet to enable efficient inference on mobile devices
maintains good representational power while using far fewer parameters than equivalent standard convolutions
forms the core design element of many efficient CNN backbones

concepts.name: Atrous (Dilated) Convolution
bullets: is a convolution that inserts gaps (dilations) between kernel elements to enlarge the receptive field
allows the network to capture larger context without increasing the number of parameters
controls the spacing of sampled input positions via a dilation rate hyperparameter
is widely used in segmentation models like DeepLab to balance resolution and context
can be combined at multiple dilation rates to form Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling

concepts.name: Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP)
bullets: is a module that applies multiple parallel atrous convolutions with different dilation rates to the same feature map
captures multi-scale context by aggregating features at several receptive field sizes
often includes an image-level pooling branch to incorporate global context
concatenates the outputs of all branches and fuses them with a 1x1 convolution
is a central component of DeepLab models that improves segmentation accuracy on complex scenes

concepts.name: PyTorch Tensor
bullets: is the core multi-dimensional array structure used to represent data and model parameters in PyTorch
stores data with a shape, dtype (such as float32), and device (CPU or GPU)
supports automatic differentiation when requires_grad is True, enabling backpropagation through computation graphs
typically uses the [N, C, H, W] layout for image data in vision models
can be reshaped, permuted, and broadcasted using tensor operations like view, permute, and unsqueeze
shares many conceptual similarities with NumPy arrays but integrates tightly with GPU acceleration and autograd

concepts.name: NumPy Array
bullets: is a fundamental n-dimensional array type provided by the NumPy library in Python
stores data in contiguous memory with a shape and dtype, supporting vectorized operations
does not natively track gradients or support automatic differentiation
is often used in pre-processing, evaluation scripts, and lab utilities around deep learning models
shares broadcasting and indexing semantics that are conceptually mirrored in PyTorch tensors
can be converted to PyTorch tensors and back when coordinating between numeric code and deep learning code

concepts.name: Paired Geometric Augmentations for Segmentation
bullets: are augmentation operations that apply the exact same random geometric transform to both the input image and its segmentation mask
include operations such as random horizontal flips, rotations, and crops applied in a synchronized fashion
are necessary to maintain spatial alignment between the image content and pixel-level labels
prevent supervision from becoming corrupted by misaligned labels during training
are typically implemented by generating random transform parameters once and using them for both image and mask

concepts.name: Precision Envelope Correction for AP
bullets: is a post-processing step applied to the precision-recall curve before computing Average Precision
enforces that precision values become a monotonically non-increasing function of recall from left to right
is implemented by scanning precision values from the highest recall backward and replacing each value with the maximum of itself and all following values
smooths out small local dips in precision caused by ranking noise or limited sample statistics
leads to a more stable and standardized AP metric consistent with common detection benchmarks

concepts.name: One-to-One Ground Truth Matching for AP
bullets: is a rule that each ground-truth object can be matched to at most one predicted bounding box when computing detection metrics
ensures that multiple high-overlap detections for the same object do not yield multiple True Positives
prevents artificial inflation of precision and recall by duplicate detections
is typically implemented by assigning predictions to ground truths greedily in descending order of confidence and IoU
is crucial for fair and consistent evaluation of object detectors

concepts.name: Attention Matrix Interpretation
bullets: refers to how the entries of the self-attention matrix A with shape [B, T, T] should be interpreted
represents for each sample b and each query token i the distribution of attention weights over all key tokens j
assigns A[b, i, j] as the relative importance that token i places on token j when aggregating contextual information
ensures that rows of the attention matrix sum to 1 after Softmax along the key dimension
provides a way to visualize which tokens attend to which other tokens in a sequence or image patch grid

concepts.name: Linear Classifier Weight Visualization
bullets: is a technique where rows of a linear classifier weight matrix are reshaped into image shapes and visualized
shows each class weight vector as a spatial template that scores similarity with input images via a dot product
highlights that a linear classifier effectively matches inputs against learned class-specific patterns
provides intuition about which regions and colors contribute positively or negatively to each class score
is particularly illustrative when applied to small images like CIFAR-10

concepts.name: NumPy Slicing Views vs Copies
bullets: distinguishes between array views that share memory with the original array and copies that own separate memory
notes that basic slicing (using : and ranges) typically returns a view sharing underlying memory
implies that modifying a slice that is a view also changes the corresponding elements in the original array
requires care when writing code to avoid unintentional side effects from shared memory
can be controlled by explicitly calling copy() when an independent array is needed

concepts.name: NumPy Reduction Operations (axis argument)
bullets: refer to operations like sum, mean, and max that aggregate values along specified axes
use the axis argument to determine which dimension is reduced while keeping others intact
produce outputs whose shape has the reduced axes removed or set to size 1 depending on keepdims
are essential for implementing metrics and losses that operate along batch, spatial, or channel dimensions
mirror similar reduction functions used in PyTorch tensor operations
